## AN IMAGE IS WORTH 16X16 WORDS: TRANSFORMERS FOR IMAGE RECOGNITION AT SCALE

**Alexey Dosovitskiy** _[∗][,][†]_ **, Lucas Beyer** _[∗]_ **, Alexander Kolesnikov** _[∗]_ **, Dirk Weissenborn** _[∗]_ **, Xiaohua Zhai** _[∗]_ **, Thomas Unterthiner, Mostafa Dehghani, Matthias Minderer, Georg Heigold, Sylvain Gelly, Jakob Uszkoreit, Neil Houlsby** _[∗][,][†]_

> _∗_ equal technical contribution, _†_ equal advising Google Research, Brain Team

_{_ adosovitskiy, neilhoulsby _}_ @google.com

## ABSTRACT

While the Transformer architecture has become the de-facto standard for natural language processing tasks, its applications to computer vision remain limited. In vision, attention is either applied in conjunction with convolutional networks, or used to replace certain components of convolutional networks while keeping their overall structure in place. We show that this reliance on CNNs is not necessary and a pure transformer applied directly to sequences of image patches can perform very well on image classification tasks. When pre-trained on large amounts of data and transferred to multiple mid-sized or small image recognition benchmarks (ImageNet, CIFAR-100, VTAB, etc.), Vision Transformer (ViT) attains excellent results compared to state-of-the-art convolutional networks while requiring substantially fewer computational resources to train.[1]

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Self-attention-based architectures, in particular Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017), have become the model of choice in natural language processing (NLP). The dominant approach is to pre-train on a large text corpus and then fine-tune on a smaller task-specific dataset (Devlin et al., 2019). Thanks to Transformers’ computational efficiency and scalability, it has become possible to train models of unprecedented size, with over 100B parameters (Brown et al., 2020; Lepikhin et al., 2020). With the models and datasets growing, there is still no sign of saturating performance.

In computer vision, however, convolutional architectures remain dominant (LeCun et al., 1989; Krizhevsky et al., 2012; He et al., 2016). Inspired by NLP successes, multiple works try combining CNN-like architectures with self-attention (Wang et al., 2018; Carion et al., 2020), some replacing the convolutions entirely (Ramachandran et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020a). The latter models, while theoretically efficient, have not yet been scaled effectively on modern hardware accelerators due to the use of specialized attention patterns. Therefore, in large-scale image recognition, classic ResNetlike architectures are still state of the art (Mahajan et al., 2018; Xie et al., 2020; Kolesnikov et al., 2020).

Inspired by the Transformer scaling successes in NLP, we experiment with applying a standard Transformer directly to images, with the fewest possible modifications. To do so, we split an image into patches and provide the sequence of linear embeddings of these patches as an input to a Transformer. Image patches are treated the same way as tokens (words) in an NLP application. We train the model on image classification in supervised fashion.

When trained on mid-sized datasets such as ImageNet without strong regularization, these models yield modest accuracies of a few percentage points below ResNets of comparable size. This seemingly discouraging outcome may be expected: Transformers lack some of the inductive biases

inherent to CNNs, such as translation equivariance and locality, and therefore do not generalize well

However, the picture changes if the models are trained on larger datasets (14M-300M images). We find that large scale training trumps inductive bias. Our Vision Transformer (ViT) attains excellent results when pre-trained at sufficient scale and transferred to tasks with fewer datapoints. When pre-trained on the public ImageNet-21k dataset or the in-house JFT-300M dataset, ViT approaches or beats state of the art on multiple image recognition benchmarks. In particular, the best model reaches the accuracy of 88 _._ 55% on ImageNet, 90 _._ 72% on ImageNet-ReaL, 94 _._ 55% on CIFAR-100, and 77 _._ 63% on the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Transformers were proposed by Vaswani et al. (2017) for machine translation, and have since become the state of the art method in many NLP tasks. Large Transformer-based models are often pre-trained on large corpora and then fine-tuned for the task at hand: BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) uses a denoising self-supervised pre-training task, while the GPT line of work uses language modeling as its pre-training task (Radford et al., 2018; 2019; Brown et al., 2020).

Naive application of self-attention to images would require that each pixel attends to every other pixel. With quadratic cost in the number of pixels, this does not scale to realistic input sizes. Thus, to apply Transformers in the context of image processing, several approximations have been tried in the past. Parmar et al. (2018) applied the self-attention only in local neighborhoods for each query pixel instead of globally. Such local multi-head dot-product self attention blocks can completely replace convolutions (Hu et al., 2019; Ramachandran et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020). In a different line of work, Sparse Transformers (Child et al., 2019) employ scalable approximations to global selfattention in order to be applicable to images. An alternative way to scale attention is to apply it in blocks of varying sizes (Weissenborn et al., 2019), in the extreme case only along individual axes (Ho et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020a). Many of these specialized attention architectures demonstrate promising results on computer vision tasks, but require complex engineering to be implemented efficiently on hardware accelerators.

Most related to ours is the model of Cordonnier et al. (2020), which extracts patches of size 2 _×_ 2 from the input image and applies full self-attention on top. This model is very similar to ViT, but our work goes further to demonstrate that large scale pre-training makes vanilla transformers competitive with (or even better than) state-of-the-art CNNs. Moreover, Cordonnier et al. (2020) use a small patch size of 2 _×_ 2 pixels, which makes the model applicable only to small-resolution images, while we handle medium-resolution images as well.

There has also been a lot of interest in combining convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with forms of self-attention, e.g. by augmenting feature maps for image classification (Bello et al., 2019) or by further processing the output of a CNN using self-attention, e.g. for object detection (Hu et al., 2018; Carion et al., 2020), video processing (Wang et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2019), image classification (Wu et al., 2020), unsupervised object discovery (Locatello et al., 2020), or unified text-vision tasks (Chen et al., 2020c; Lu et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019).

Another recent related model is image GPT (iGPT) (Chen et al., 2020a), which applies Transformers to image pixels after reducing image resolution and color space. The model is trained in an unsupervised fashion as a generative model, and the resulting representation can then be fine-tuned or probed linearly for classification performance, achieving a maximal accuracy of 72% on ImageNet.

Our work adds to the increasing collection of papers that explore image recognition at larger scales than the standard ImageNet dataset. The use of additional data sources allows to achieve state-ofthe-art results on standard benchmarks (Mahajan et al., 2018; Touvron et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2020). Moreover, Sun et al. (2017) study how CNN performance scales with dataset size, and Kolesnikov et al. (2020); Djolonga et al. (2020) perform an empirical exploration of CNN transfer learning from large scale datasets such as ImageNet-21k and JFT-300M. We focus on these two latter datasets as well, but train Transformers instead of ResNet-based models used in prior works.

## 3 METHOD

In model design we follow the original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) as closely as possible. An advantage of this intentionally simple setup is that scalable NLP Transformer architectures – and their efficient implementations – can be used almost out of the box.

## 3.1 VISION TRANSFORMER (VIT)

An overview of the model is depicted in Figure 1. The standard Transformer receives as input a 1D sequence of token embeddings. To handle 2D images, we reshape the image **x** _∈_ R _[H][×][W][ ×][C]_ into a sequence of flattened 2D patches **x** _p ∈_ R _[N][×]_[(] _[P]_[ 2] _[·][C]_[)] , where ( _H, W_ ) is the resolution of the original image, _C_ is the number of channels, ( _P, P_ ) is the resolution of each image patch, and _N_ = _HW/P_[2] is the resulting number of patches, which also serves as the effective input sequence length for the Transformer. The Transformer uses constant latent vector size _D_ through all of its layers, so we flatten the patches and map to _D_ dimensions with a trainable linear projection (Eq. 1). We refer to the output of this projection as the patch embeddings.

Similar to BERT’s [class] token, we prepend a learnable embedding to the sequence of embedded patches ( **z**[0] 0[=] **[x]**[class][),][whose][state at the output of the Transformer encoder (] **[z]**[0] _L_[) serves as the] image representation **y** (Eq. 4). Both during pre-training and fine-tuning, a classification head is attached to **z**[0] _L_[. The classification head is implemented by a MLP with one hidden layer at pre-training] time and by a single linear layer at fine-tuning time.

Position embeddings are added to the patch embeddings to retain positional information. We use standard learnable 1D position embeddings, since we have not observed significant performance gains from using more advanced 2D-aware position embeddings (Appendix D.3). The resulting sequence of embedding vectors serves as input to the encoder.

The Transformer encoder (Vaswani et al., 2017) consists of alternating layers of multiheaded selfattention (MSA, see Appendix A) and MLP blocks (Eq. 2, 3). Layernorm (LN) is applied before every block, and residual connections after every block (Wang et al., 2019; Baevski & Auli, 2019).

The MLP contains two layers with a GELU non-linearity.

**Inductive bias.** We note that Vision Transformer has much less image-specific inductive bias than CNNs. In CNNs, locality, two-dimensional neighborhood structure, and translation equivariance are baked into each layer throughout the whole model. In ViT, only MLP layers are local and translationally equivariant, while the self-attention layers are global. The two-dimensional neighborhood structure is used very sparingly: in the beginning of the model by cutting the image into patches and at fine-tuning time for adjusting the position embeddings for images of different resolution (as described below). Other than that, the position embeddings at initialization time carry no information about the 2D positions of the patches and all spatial relations between the patches have to be learned from scratch.

**Hybrid Architecture.** As an alternative to raw image patches, the input sequence can be formed from feature maps of a CNN (LeCun et al., 1989). In this hybrid model, the patch embedding projection **E** (Eq. 1) is applied to patches extracted from a CNN feature map. As a special case, the patches can have spatial size 1x1, which means that the input sequence is obtained by simply flattening the spatial dimensions of the feature map and projecting to the Transformer dimension. The classification input embedding and position embeddings are added as described above.

## 3.2 FINE-TUNING AND HIGHER RESOLUTION

Typically, we pre-train ViT on large datasets, and fine-tune to (smaller) downstream tasks. For this, we remove the pre-trained prediction head and attach a zero-initialized _D × K_ feedforward layer, where _K_ is the number of downstream classes. It is often beneficial to fine-tune at higher resolution than pre-training (Touvron et al., 2019; Kolesnikov et al., 2020). When feeding images of higher resolution, we keep the patch size the same, which results in a larger effective sequence length. The Vision Transformer can handle arbitrary sequence lengths (up to memory constraints), however, the pre-trained position embeddings may no longer be meaningful. We therefore perform 2D interpolation of the pre-trained position embeddings, according to their location in the original image. Note that this resolution adjustment and patch extraction are the only points at which an inductive bias about the 2D structure of the images is manually injected into the Vision Transformer.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We evaluate the representation learning capabilities of ResNet, Vision Transformer (ViT), and the hybrid. To understand the data requirements of each model, we pre-train on datasets of varying size and evaluate many benchmark tasks. When considering the computational cost of pre-training the model, ViT performs very favourably, attaining state of the art on most recognition benchmarks at a lower pre-training cost. Lastly, we perform a small experiment using self-supervision, and show that self-supervised ViT holds promise for the future.

## 4.1 SETUP

**Datasets.** To explore model scalability, we use the ILSVRC-2012 ImageNet dataset with 1k classes and 1.3M images (we refer to it as ImageNet in what follows), its superset ImageNet-21k with 21k classes and 14M images (Deng et al., 2009), and JFT (Sun et al., 2017) with 18k classes and 303M high-resolution images. We de-duplicate the pre-training datasets w.r.t. the test sets of the downstream tasks following Kolesnikov et al. (2020). We transfer the models trained on these dataset to several benchmark tasks: ImageNet on the original validation labels and the cleaned-up ReaL labels (Beyer et al., 2020), CIFAR-10/100 (Krizhevsky, 2009), Oxford-IIIT Pets (Parkhi et al., 2012), and Oxford Flowers-102 (Nilsback & Zisserman, 2008). For these datasets, pre-processing follows Kolesnikov et al. (2020).

Table 1: Details of Vision Transformer model variants.

We also evaluate on the 19-task VTAB classification suite (Zhai et al., 2019b). VTAB evaluates low-data transfer to diverse tasks, using 1 000 training examples per task. The tasks are divided into three groups: _Natural_ – tasks like the above, Pets, CIFAR, etc. _Specialized_ – medical and satellite imagery, and _Structured_ – tasks that require geometric understanding like localization.

**Model Variants.** We base ViT configurations on those used for BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), as summarized in Table 1. The “Base” and “Large” models are directly adopted from BERT and we add the larger “Huge” model. In what follows we use brief notation to indicate the model size and the input patch size: for instance, ViT-L/16 means the “Large” variant with 16 _×_ 16 input patch size. Note that the Transformer’s sequence length is inversely proportional to the square of the patch size, thus models with smaller patch size are computationally more expensive.

For the baseline CNNs, we use ResNet (He et al., 2016), but replace the Batch Normalization layers (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015) with Group Normalization (Wu & He, 2018), and used standardized convolutions (Qiao et al., 2019). These modifications improve transfer (Kolesnikov et al., 2020), and we denote the modified model “ResNet (BiT)”. For the hybrids, we feed the intermediate feature maps into ViT with patch size of one “pixel”. To experiment with different sequence lengths, we either (i) take the output of stage 4 of a regular ResNet50 or (ii) remove stage 4, place the same number of layers in stage 3 (keeping the total number of layers), and take the output of this extended stage 3. Option (ii) results in a 4x longer sequence length, and a more expensive ViT model.

**Training & Fine-tuning.** We train all models, including ResNets, using Adam (Kingma & Ba, 2015) with _β_ 1 = 0 _._ 9, _β_ 2 = 0 _._ 999, a batch size of 4096 and apply a high weight decay of 0 _._ 1, which we found to be useful for transfer of all models (Appendix D.1 shows that, in contrast to common practices, Adam works slightly better than SGD for ResNets in our setting). We use a linear learning rate warmup and decay, see Appendix B.1 for details. For fine-tuning we use SGD with momentum, batch size 512, for all models, see Appendix B.1.1. For ImageNet results in Table 2, we fine-tuned at higher resolution: 512 for ViT-L/16 and 518 for ViT-H/14, and also used Polyak & Juditsky (1992) averaging with a factor of 0 _._ 9999 (Ramachandran et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020b).

**Metrics.** We report results on downstream datasets either through few-shot or fine-tuning accuracy. Fine-tuning accuracies capture the performance of each model after fine-tuning it on the respective dataset. Few-shot accuracies are obtained by solving a regularized least-squares regression problem that maps the (frozen) representation of a subset of training images to _{−_ 1 _,_ 1 _}[K]_ target vectors. This formulation allows us to recover the exact solution in closed form. Though we mainly focus on fine-tuning performance, we sometimes use linear few-shot accuracies for fast on-the-fly evaluation where fine-tuning would be too costly.

## 4.2 COMPARISON TO STATE OF THE ART

We first compare our largest models – ViT-H/14 and ViT-L/16 – to state-of-the-art CNNs from the literature. The first comparison point is Big Transfer (BiT) (Kolesnikov et al., 2020), which performs supervised transfer learning with large ResNets. The second is Noisy Student (Xie et al., 2020), which is a large EfficientNet trained using semi-supervised learning on ImageNet and JFT300M with the labels removed. Currently, Noisy Student is the state of the art on ImageNet and BiT-L on the other datasets reported here. All models were trained on TPUv3 hardware, and we report the number of TPUv3-core-days taken to pre-train each of them, that is, the number of TPU v3 cores (2 per chip) used for training multiplied by the training time in days.

Table 2 shows the results. The smaller ViT-L/16 model pre-trained on JFT-300M outperforms BiT-L (which is pre-trained on the same dataset) on all tasks, while requiring substantially less computational resources to train. The larger model, ViT-H/14, further improves the performance, especially on the more challenging datasets – ImageNet, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite. Interestingly, this

ViT-H/14 outperforms BiT-R152x4, and other methods, on the _Natural_ and _Structured_ tasks. On the _Specialized_ the performance of the top two models is similar.

## 4.3 PRE-TRAINING DATA REQUIREMENTS

The Vision Transformer performs well when pre-trained on a large JFT-300M dataset. With fewer inductive biases for vision than ResNets, how crucial is the dataset size? We perform two series of experiments.

First, we pre-train ViT models on datasets of increasing size: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT300M. To boost the performance on the smaller datasets, we optimize three basic regularization parameters – weight decay, dropout, and label smoothing. Figure 3 shows the results after finetuning to ImageNet (results on other datasets are shown in Table 5)[2] . When pre-trained on the smallest dataset, ImageNet, ViT-Large models underperform compared to ViT-Base models, despite (moderate) regularization. With ImageNet-21k pre-training, their performances are similar. Only with JFT-300M, do we see the full benefit of larger models. Figure 3 also shows the performance

Second, we train our models on random subsets of 9M, 30M, and 90M as well as the full JFT300M dataset. We do not perform additional regularization on the smaller subsets and use the same hyper-parameters for all settings. This way, we assess the intrinsic model properties, and not the effect of regularization. We do, however, use early-stopping, and report the best validation accuracy achieved during training. To save compute, we report few-shot linear accuracy instead of full finetuning accuracy. Figure 4 contains the results. Vision Transformers overfit more than ResNets with comparable computational cost on smaller datasets. For example, ViT-B/32 is slightly faster than ResNet50; it performs much worse on the 9M subset, but better on 90M+ subsets. The same is true for ResNet152x2 and ViT-L/16. This result reinforces the intuition that the convolutional inductive bias is useful for smaller datasets, but for larger ones, learning the relevant patterns directly from data is sufficient, even beneficial.

Overall, the few-shot results on ImageNet (Figure 4), as well as the low-data results on VTAB (Table 2) seem promising for very low-data transfer. Further analysis of few-shot properties of ViT is an exciting direction of future work.

## 4.4 SCALING STUDY

We perform a controlled scaling study of different models by evaluating transfer performance from JFT-300M. In this setting data size does not bottleneck the models’ performances, and we assess performance versus pre-training cost of each model. The model set includes: 7 ResNets, R50x1, R50x2 R101x1, R152x1, R152x2, pre-trained for 7 epochs, plus R152x2 and R200x3 pre-trained for 14 epochs; 6 Vision Transformers, ViT-B/32, B/16, L/32, L/16, pre-trained for 7 epochs, plus L/16 and H/14 pre-trained for 14 epochs; and 5 hybrids, R50+ViT-B/32, B/16, L/32, L/16 pretrained for 7 epochs, plus R50+ViT-L/16 pre-trained for 14 epochs (for hybrids, the number at the end of the model name stands not for the patch size, but for the total dowsampling ratio in the ResNet backbone).

Figure 5 contains the transfer performance versus total pre-training compute (see Appendix D.4 for details on computational costs). Detailed results per model are provided in Table 6 in the Appendix. A few patterns can be observed. First, Vision Transformers dominate ResNets on the performance/compute trade-off. ViT uses approximately 2 _−_ 4 _×_ less compute to attain the same performance (average over 5 datasets). Second, hybrids slightly outperform ViT at small computational budgets, but the difference vanishes for larger models. This result is somewhat surprising, since one might expect convolutional local feature processing to assist ViT at any size. Third, Vision Transformers appear not to saturate within the range tried, motivating future scaling efforts.

## 4.5 INSPECTING VISION TRANSFORMER

To begin to understand how the Vision Transformer processes image data, we analyze its internal representations. The first layer of the Vision Transformer linearly projects the flattened patches into a lower-dimensional space (Eq. 1). Figure 7 (left) shows the top principal components of the the learned embedding filters. The components resemble plausible basis functions for a low-dimensional representation of the fine structure within each patch.

After the projection, a learned position embedding is added to the patch representations. Figure 7 (center) shows that the model learns to encode distance within the image in the similarity of position embeddings, i.e. closer patches tend to have more similar position embeddings. Further, the row-column structure appears; patches in the same row/column have similar embeddings. Finally, a sinusoidal structure is sometimes apparent for larger grids (Appendix D). That the position embeddings learn to represent 2D image topology explains why hand-crafted 2D-aware embedding variants do not yield improvements (Appendix D.3).

Self-attention allows ViT to integrate information across the entire image even in the lowest layers. We investigate to what degree the network makes use of this capability. Specifically, we compute the average distance in image space across which information is integrated, based on the attention weights (Figure 7, right). This “attention distance” is analogous to receptive field size in CNNs.

We find that some heads attend to most of the image already in the lowest layers, showing that the ability to integrate information globally is indeed used by the model. Other attention heads have consistently small attention distances in the low layers. This highly localized attention is less pronounced in hybrid models that apply a ResNet before the Transformer (Figure 7, right), suggesting that it may serve a similar function as early convolutional layers in CNNs. Further, the attention distance increases with network depth. Globally, we find that the model attends to image regions that are semantically relevant for classification (Figure 6).

With self-supervised pre-training, our smaller ViT-B/16 model achieves 79.9% accuracy on ImageNet, a significant improvement of 2% to training from scratch, but still 4% behind supervised pre-training. Appendix B.1.2 contains further details. We leave exploration of contrastive pre-training (Chen et al., 2020b; He et al., 2020; Bachman et al., 2019; H´enaff et al., 2020) to future work.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We have explored the direct application of Transformers to image recognition. Unlike prior works using self-attention in computer vision, we do not introduce image-specific inductive biases into the architecture apart from the initial patch extraction step. Instead, we interpret an image as a sequence of patches and process it by a standard Transformer encoder as used in NLP. This simple, yet scalable, strategy works surprisingly well when coupled with pre-training on large datasets. Thus, Vision Transformer matches or exceeds the state of the art on many image classification datasets, whilst being relatively cheap to pre-train.

While these initial results are encouraging, many challenges remain. One is to apply ViT to other computer vision tasks, such as detection and segmentation. Our results, coupled with those in Carion et al. (2020), indicate the promise of this approach. Another challenge is to continue exploring selfsupervised pre-training methods. Our initial experiments show improvement from self-supervised pre-training, but there is still large gap between self-supervised and large-scale supervised pretraining. Finally, further scaling of ViT would likely lead to improved performance.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and potentially transformative shift in computer vision by demonstrating that the standard Transformer architecture—largely unmodified from its NLP origins—can outperform state-of-the-art Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) when scaled to sufficiently large datasets. The quality of the work is high, characterized by a clear, bold hypothesis and a rigorous experimental design that addresses the lack of inherent inductive biases (like translation equivariance) through massive pre-training on datasets like JFT-300M. The clarity of the writing is excellent; the authors provide a transparent comparison to existing hybrid models and justify their "vanilla" approach by emphasizing computational efficiency on modern hardware. However, a significant issue—and a potential barrier to broad adoption—is the extreme reliance on massive, private datasets and vast "Google-scale" computational resources, which makes the results difficult for the wider academic community to replicate or build upon without access to similar infrastructure. Additionally, the paper’s finding that ViT underperforms on mid-sized datasets like ImageNet emphasizes that while the model is scalable, it is significantly less "data-efficient" than CNNs, a limitation that warrants deeper discussion regarding the practical applicability of the model for niche or data-constrained domains.

comments.title: Truncated sentence regarding generalization
comments.quote: Transformers lack some of the inductive biases inherent to CNNs, such as translation equivariance and locality, and therefore do not generalize well
comments.explanation: The sentence ends abruptly. Based on the context of the paragraph, the authors intend to explain that Transformers do not generalize well when trained on insufficient data (mid-sized datasets like ImageNet) because they must learn spatial relationships from scratch. While the meaning is recoverable from the following paragraph ('However, the picture changes if the models are trained on larger datasets'), the current passage leaves a causal claim ('therefore do not generalize well') hanging without its necessary qualification (e.g., '...when trained on insufficient data').

comments.title: Ambiguous performance claim for iGPT
comments.quote: achieving a maximal accuracy of 72% on ImageNet.
comments.explanation: In Section 2, the authors mention iGPT achieves 72% on ImageNet. While technically accurate for the linear probe results of iGPT-L, the paper overall argues that ViT is superior because it scales better. However, without specifying whether this 72% refers to top-1 or top-5 accuracy, or acknowledging that iGPT's primary goal was generative/unsupervised, a reader might conflate this with the 88.55% supervised/fine-tuned result of ViT elsewhere in the paper. Since this is in the Related Work section and serves primarily to contrast approaches, it is not a major failure of methodology, but it is underspecified compared to the precise metrics used for ViT.

In standard matrix notation, $\mathbf{E}$ would typically be a projection matrix of size $(P^2C) \times D$. If the ResNet-50 backbone has a total downsampling ratio of 16, a $224 \times 224$ image results in a $14 \times 14$ feature map. Feeding $1 \times 1$ 'patches' of this map into the Transformer results in a sequence length of 196. This is mathematically identical to taking the original image, using $16 \times 16$ patches, and ignoring the CNN. While the logic holds, the nomenclature 'patch size of one pixel' in Section 3.1 combined with the 'downsampling ratio' explanation in 4.4 might be confusing: a '16' in a pure ViT-L/16 refers to a $16 \times 16$ spatial slice of pixels, whereas in a hybrid, it refers to a $1 \times 1$ slice of a feature map that has already been downsampled by 16. The resulting sequence lengths are the same, but the 'patch' units are conceptually different (pixels vs. deep feature vectors).

For a model that relies heavily on learned 1D embeddings to prove that it can learn spatial structure from scratch, the choice of interpolation (linear, bicubic, or nearest-neighbor) is non-trivial when moving to higher resolutions. While bicubic is standard in many vision pipelines, the specific mechanism for how 1D learnable vectors (which have no inherent 2D coordinates) are assigned 'locations' for 2D interpolation is only briefly mentioned as 'according to their location in the original image'. This assumes the 1D sequence $[1, 2, ..., N]$ is mapped to a grid $[1,1]...[P,P]$ as a prior, which is a specific form of 2D inductive bias that the paper claims to minimize.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and technically sound contribution to the field of computer vision by demonstrating that a pure Transformer architecture—when scaled appropriately—can outperform state-of-the-art convolutional networks on image recognition tasks. The quality of the work is high, with a clear narrative arc that motivates the design of the Vision Transformer (ViT) through analogy to NLP, systematically challenges the necessity of convolutional inductive biases, and provides strong empirical evidence that large-scale pre-training compensates for their absence. The clarity of exposition is excellent: the introduction effectively frames the central hypothesis, the related work is comprehensive and well-contextualized, and the core idea of treating image patches as tokens is communicated intuitively. However, the most significant issue lies in the incompleteness of the submitted text—specifically, the abrupt cutoff at the end of Section 2, which truncates the discussion of prior scaling studies and omits the entire Methods, Experiments, and Conclusion sections. As a result, critical details such as model architecture specifications, training protocols, ablation studies, and quantitative benchmarks are missing, making it impossible to fully assess the validity or reproducibility of the claimed results. While the abstract and introduction strongly suggest a transformative finding—that Transformers can succeed in vision without convolutions given sufficient scale—the lack of experimental substantiation in this partial submission limits its scientific completeness. For a full review, one would need the full paper to verify whether the conclusions are well-supported by data and whether computational costs, data efficiency, and architectural choices are fairly analyzed. Nonetheless, the conceptual message—that scale can substitute for inductive bias—is both provocative and potentially influential, provided it is backed by rigorous experiments in the complete manuscript.

comments.title: Ambiguity in patch size notation and resolution handling
comments.quote: we handle medium-resolution images as well
comments.explanation: The term 'medium-resolution' is vague and lacks a precise definition. While context suggests it refers to standard ImageNet-scale resolutions (~224–600px), this is not explicitly stated. The phrasing risks misleading readers into thinking patch size fundamentally limits resolution, when in fact it only affects sequence length due to position embedding interpolation. Although the method is correctly described later (Section 3.2), the initial ambiguity could confuse readers unfamiliar with Vision Transformers. The core claim—superior generalization compared to 2×2-patch models—is valid and well-supported.

comments.title: Under-specified position embedding interpolation method
comments.quote: We therefore perform 2D interpolation of the pre-trained position embeddings, according to their location in the original image.
comments.explanation: While the use of 2D interpolation for position embeddings during fine-tuning is critical for resolution scaling, the specific method (e.g., bilinear, nearest-neighbor) is not specified in the main text. The mapping of patch coordinates to continuous space and interpolation details are omitted, which affects reproducibility. Although Appendix D.3 likely contains these details and such techniques are now standard (e.g., DeiT uses bilinear), the lack of clarity in the main method description is notable given that this is one of the few inductive biases used in ViT. This is a moderate shortcoming in exposition.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for using 1D position embeddings over 2D-aware variants
comments.quote: We use standard learnable 1D position embeddings, since we have not observed significant performance gains from using more advanced 2D-aware position embeddings (Appendix D.3).
comments.explanation: The decision to use 1D over 2D-aware position embeddings is empirically justified but lacks intuitive explanation in the main text. Given that images are inherently 2D, a reader might expect stronger inductive biases or spatial awareness. The claim relies entirely on an appendix with no summary of what 'significant' means or which 2D-aware methods were tested (e.g., axial, relative, sinusoidal). A brief rationale—such as 'self-attention effectively learns 2D structure from linear ordering'—would improve transparency. While the choice is valid and backed by results, the justification is undersold and could leave readers skeptical.

comments.title: Ambiguity and inconsistency in model notation: ViT-B vs. ViT-b
comments.quote: ViT-b/32 and ViT-B/32 used in scaling comparisons with ResNets and hybrids.
comments.explanation: The paper introduces 'ViT-b' as a variant of 'ViT-B' with all hidden dimensions halved, but this notation creates confusion due to the reliance on case sensitivity ('B' vs. 'b'). The distinction is defined only in a figure caption (Figure 4), not in the main model description or Table 1, making it easy to miss. This risks misinterpretation—especially in figures like Figure 3 where both appear—and complicates reproducibility. While context eventually clarifies the meaning, the use of visually similar labels for architecturally distinct models increases cognitive load and may lead to errors in parsing. A more distinguishable nomenclature (e.g., ViT-S for 'small') would have been preferable.

comments.title: Unjustified exclusion of Noisy Student from compute comparison
comments.quote: However, we note that pre-training efficiency may be affected not only by the architecture choice, but also other parameters, such as training schedule, optimizer, weight decay, etc. We provide a controlled study of performance vs. compute for different architectures in Section 4.4.
comments.explanation: The paper highlights ViT's computational efficiency (0.68k vs. 9.9k–12.3k TPUv3-core-days) relative to BiT and Noisy Student, but Noisy Student uses semi-supervised learning while ViT and BiT use fully supervised training on labeled data. This training paradigm difference significantly affects both compute and data requirements. While Section 4.4 provides a controlled comparison limited to supervised models (excluding Noisy Student), Table 2 includes Noisy Student in accuracy results without a parallel caveat about training methodology. This juxtaposition risks implying a fairer comparison than exists, potentially misleading readers into thinking ViT is more efficient across all regimes. The authors are technically correct in separation, but the presentation weakens the validity of one of the paper’s central claims.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of patch size in hybrid architecture
comments.quote: For the hybrids, we feed the intermediate feature maps into ViT with patch size of one “pixel”.
comments.explanation: The term 'patch size of one pixel' is misleading when applied to CNN feature maps, which consist of activation units, not pixels. While the quotes around 'pixel' suggest metaphorical use, a reader might interpret this as operating on raw image pixels. The correct interpretation—each spatial location in the feature map becomes a token—is clarified later, and 1×1 patches are described as a special case for flattening. Still, the overloading of 'patch size' (from image pixels to feature map regions) without disambiguation could confuse those unfamiliar with hybrid models. This is a mild ambiguity but recoverable from context.

comments.title: Inconsistent precision in reported accuracy values between text and table
comments.quote: The best model reaches the accuracy of 88 _._ 55% on ImageNet, 90 _._ 72% on ImageNet-ReaL, 94 _._ 55% on CIFAR-100, and 77 _._ 63% on the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.
comments.explanation: The text reports accuracy values with high precision (e.g., 88.55%) as definitive, but omits the uncertainty (±0.04) present in Table 2 and the footnote stating results are averaged over three runs. This risks implying deterministic performance. While the values match Table 2 and such summaries are common in introductions, the lack of uncertainty qualification in the initial claim constitutes a minor overstatement that could mislead readers about result stability.

comments.title: Potential OCR and formatting artifacts in model names and equations
comments.quote: |(ViT-H/14)|(ViT-L/16)|(ViT-L/16)|(ResNet152x4)|(EffcientNet-L2)|
comments.explanation: Several instances of likely OCR or formatting issues appear: 'EffcientNet-L2' (missing 'i'), 'VIVI-Ex-100% (R5 0x3)' with a space in 'R50x3', inconsistent capitalization ('ViT-b/32'), and corrupted math notation (e.g., 'R^[N×(P²·C)]' with misplaced brackets). These do not introduce conceptual errors—the intended models and dimensions are recoverable from context and known naming conventions—but they create minor confusion and reduce clarity. The most problematic is the 'VIVI' model, which appears to be a misread of 'BiT-R50x3' or similar. While none impair understanding, the accumulation of such artifacts slightly degrades professionalism and readability, especially in figures and tables.

comments.title: Conflation of few-shot and low-data fine-tuning evaluation regimes
comments.quote: Overall, the few-shot results on ImageNet (Figure 4), as well as the low-data results on VTAB (Table 2) seem promising for very low-data transfer.
comments.explanation: The authors suggest both few-shot (frozen features + linear probe) and VTAB (fine-tuned with 1,000 examples per task) demonstrate strong low-data transfer. While both are low-data compared to full fine-tuning, conflating them may mislead readers into thinking ViT excels in extreme few-shot settings (e.g., 5 examples) across datasets. The VTAB results are from fine-tuned models, not few-shot evaluation. The distinction is technically maintained, but the prose blurs the regimes. The claim would be stronger if few-shot performance were also shown for VTAB. This is a minor issue in framing, but risks overstating alignment between evaluation protocols.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Figure 3 axis labeling regarding pre-training dataset sizes
comments.quote: Number of JFT pre-training samples
comments.explanation: The x-axis in the second plot of Figure 3 is labeled 'Number of JFT pre-training samples' with tick marks at 10M, 30M, etc., but earlier text discusses pre-training on three distinct datasets: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M. This creates initial confusion: are these JFT subsets or different datasets? Context resolves it—the left plot shows cross-dataset performance, the right shows in-dataset scaling within JFT—but the transition is abrupt and poorly segmented. A clearer label such as 'Pre-training on JFT subsets' or visual distinction between plots would prevent misunderstanding. The issue is minor but reflects a missed opportunity for clarity in a key ablation figure.

comments.title: Ambiguity in patch size notation and resolution handling
comments.quote: we handle medium-resolution images as well
comments.explanation: At first, I wondered what 'medium-resolution' meant precisely, especially since the authors contrast their work with Cordonnier et al. (2020), who use 2×2 patches. The passage implies that larger patch sizes (like 16×16) allow scaling to higher-resolution inputs, which is correct. However, the term 'medium-resolution' is vague and lacks a definition. From context — particularly the discussion of fine-tuning at 512 or 518 resolution — we can infer they mean standard ImageNet-scale resolutions (~224–600px), but this is not explicit. The model's ability to handle such inputs depends on patch size and how position embeddings are interpolated, as detailed in Section 3.2. So while this is *not* a methodological error — the fine-tuning procedure *is* clearly described later — the phrasing in isolation risks misleading readers into thinking patch size fundamentally limits supported image resolution, when in fact it only affects sequence length. Nonetheless, the key idea (that ViT generalizes better than 2×2-patch models) is valid and well-supported.

comments.title: Numerical discrepancy in ImageNet accuracy claim
comments.quote: the best model reaches the accuracy of 88 _._ 55% on ImageNet
comments.explanation: This quote states a specific accuracy of 88.55% on ImageNet. However, in Table 2 (provided in the full context), we see that ViT-H/14 achieves 88.55±0.04% on ImageNet when pre-trained on JFT-300M — matching the claim. So the number itself is correct. The concern arises from the phrasing 'the best model' — could this refer to a different variant not listed? But given Table 2 reports ViT-H/14 as the best-performing variant on most benchmarks, and the accuracy aligns, the claim is justified. Furthermore, footnote [*] clarifies that Noisy Student achieves ~88.5%, confirming ViT’s competitive edge. Therefore, no actual numerical inconsistency exists. The precision in reporting (88.55%) is appropriate and matches standard reporting in vision benchmarks.

comments.title: Potential notation confusion in dimension reshaping
comments.quote: we reshape the image **x** _∈_ R _[H][×][W][ ×][C]_ into a sequence of flattened 2D patches **x** _p ∈_ R _[N][×]_[(] _[P]_[ 2] _[·][C]_[)]
comments.explanation: The reshaping equation uses non-standard LaTeX-style layout. The expression R _[N][×]_[(] _[P]_[ 2] _[·][C]_[)] appears to mean R^{N × (P²·C)}, which is correct: each patch is of size P×P×C, so flattening gives dimension P²C, and there are N patches. The notation is awkward due to OCR-like formatting (e.g., misplaced brackets), but this seems to be a rendering artifact rather than a substantive error. The description 'flattened 2D patches' aligns with standard practice in ViT. Given that the dimensionality makes sense in context and is consistent with Figure 1 and later model variants (Table 1), this is not a real issue. However, a reader unfamiliar with ViT might initially stumble at the bracket soup, though context resolves it.

comments.title: Overstatement of 'pure transformer' claim
comments.quote: a pure transformer applied directly to sequences of image patches can perform very well on image classification tasks
comments.explanation: The term 'pure transformer' implies no CNN-like components. While the model does avoid convolutions in the body, it includes a *learnable linear projection* of patches — an operation analogous to a convolutional embedding layer. Similarly, the use of a 'classification token' and position embeddings are standard NLP-inspired additions, not intrinsic to the original Transformer's encoder-only use in translation. In that sense, it's not strictly 'pure' — but this is conventional terminology in the field post-ViT to mean 'non-convolutional backend'. The authors clarify this later by contrasting with hybrids using CNN feature maps. So while the phrasing may seem slightly exaggerated to a careful reader, it aligns with widespread usage (e.g., 'vanilla Transformer'), and the contribution remains valid. Still, a reader might infer that no inductive bias is used at all, when in fact patching and position embeddings encode some spatial assumptions. This outweighs as a mild overclaim rather than a flaw.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for lack of 2D-aware position embeddings
comments.quote: since we have not observed significant performance gains from using more advanced 2D-aware position embeddings (Appendix D.3)
comments.explanation: The authors claim that 1D position embeddings are sufficient, citing an appendix. While this is plausible — empirical results back it — the main text offers no intuition for *why* 1D embeddings work well despite the 2D structure. In a field increasingly focused on geometric priors, simply stating no gains were observed may dissatisfy readers expecting deeper analysis. Appendix D.3 (external to passage) presumably addresses this, so this is not a fatal omission. However, given that the paper emphasizes low inductive bias, a brief justification (e.g., 'self-attention learns 2D patterns effectively even from linear order') would improve transparency. As-is, the claim is technically sound but undersold. This is a shortcoming in exposition, not rigor.

comments.title: Inconsistent matrix dimension notation in patch reshaping
comments.quote: we reshape the image **x** _∈_ R _[H][×][W][ ×][C]_ into a sequence of flattened 2D patches **x** _p ∈_ R _[N][×]_[(] _[P]_[ 2] _[·][C]_[)]
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned about the notation in the reshaping of the image into patches. The input image x is defined as an element of R^[H×W×C], which is standard. However, the reshaped patch sequence is written as x_p ∈ R^[N×(P²·C)], which seems correct in intent—each patch is flattened into a vector of size P²·C, and there are N such patches. But the notation R^[N×(P²·C)] mixes superscript bracketing with inline math and seems inconsistent with how tensors are typically written. More critically, the use of 'P²·C' inside brackets suggests a scalar dimension, but the parentheses placement—especially with potential OCR artifacts like '[(] [P]_[ 2]'—makes it ambiguous whether this is meant to be R^[N×(P²C)] or something else. Given that P is the patch size (e.g., 16), P²C is the flattened patch dimension, so the expression is mathematically sound. Furthermore, Table 1 confirms models use D=768, which aligns with P²C for P=16 and C=3, so the intended value is correct. Despite OCR noise, the dimensional logic holds, and the authors clearly intend standard linear embedding of flattened patches. Thus, while the notation is awkward due to formatting, it does not represent a real mathematical error.

comments.title: Ambiguity in classification token initialization and naming
comments.quote: we prepend a learnable embedding to the sequence of embedded patches ( **z**[0] 0[=] **[x]**[class][),][whose][state at the output of the Transformer encoder (] **[z]**[0] _L_[) serves as the] image representation **y** (Eq. 4)
comments.explanation: The notation here is heavily corrupted by OCR, making it difficult to parse. The expression '**z**[0] 0[=] **[x]**[class]' appears to be an attempt to write z₀⁰ = x_class or z₀ = x_class, referring to the initial state of the class token. Later, 'z₀_L' likely means the output of the class token after L layers. The intended meaning is clear from context: a learnable class token is prepended to the sequence and its final state serves as the image representation. This follows BERT's approach, which is cited. The subscript and superscript notation (layer and token index) is conventional, and the flow matches the architecture. Despite significant OCR corruption, the core idea is recoverable from surrounding prose and standard practice. No conceptual error is present.

comments.title: Under-specified position embedding interpolation method
comments.quote: We therefore perform 2D interpolation of the pre-trained position embeddings, according to their location in the original image.
comments.explanation: The passage states that 2D interpolation is used for position embeddings when fine-tuning at higher resolution, which is crucial because position embeddings are learnable and fixed in number during pre-training. However, the method of interpolation is not specified—bilinear? nearest-neighbor? how are patch coordinates mapped to continuous space? This affects reproducibility. While Appendix D.3 may contain details (implied by a forward reference), the main text lacks implementation specifics. In contrast, papers like DeiT (which builds on this work) explicitly mention bilinear interpolation. Given that resolution adjustment is highlighted as one of the only inductive biases in ViT, the omission in the main method description is notable. Still, since the authors reference an appendix and the technique is now standard, this is not a major flaw, but a moderately underspecified step.

comments.title: Missing justification for omitting 2D-aware position embeddings
comments.quote: We use standard learnable 1D position embeddings, since we have not observed significant performance gains from using more advanced 2D-aware position embeddings (Appendix D.3).
comments.explanation: The authors choose 1D position embeddings despite the 2D nature of images, which might seem counterintuitive. The justification is deferred to Appendix D.3, stating that no significant gains were observed. This is acceptable in context—many vision models use 1D embeddings with interpolation—and the choice is empirically validated. However, the main text provides no intuition or data on what constitutes 'significant', nor does it define the alternatives tested (e.g., axial, relative, or 2D-sinusoidal). A brief statement in the main paper about the types of 2D-aware embeddings tested would strengthen the rationale. Still, since the claim is backed by appendix studies and the model performs well, this is a minor framing concern.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'Hybrid Architecture' patch size
comments.quote: As a special case, the patches can have spatial size 1x1, which means that the input sequence is obtained by simply flattening the spatial dimensions of the feature map and projecting to the Transformer dimension.
comments.explanation: This is mostly clear: using 1×1 patches on a CNN feature map produces a sequence by flattening spatial dimensions. However, the term 'patch size' is overloaded—earlier it refers to image pixels (e.g., 16×16), but here it refers to CNN feature map regions. This could mislead a reader into thinking the model applies spatially larger patches to features. But context resolves this: it's explicitly called a 'special case', and the input is formed by flattening. The hybrid model is distinct from the pure ViT, so the usage is technically correct. The section is introductory to the hybrid variant, so minor ambiguity is acceptable.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of patch size in hybrid architecture
comments.quote: For the hybrids, we feed the intermediate feature maps into ViT with patch size of one “pixel”.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this statement seems to suggest that in the hybrid architecture, the ViT operates on 1x1 'pixel' patches taken directly from CNN feature maps. However, the term 'pixel' is in quotes, hinting at a potential OCR or notational issue—especially since in standard usage, feature maps do not consist of pixels but of activation units. Upon checking the full context, the paper later clarifies that for hybrids, patches are formed by flattening spatial dimensions of feature maps, and 1x1 patches are a special case meaning that each spatial location in the feature map is treated as a token. The intent is clear: spatial positions in the CNN output are treated as ViT input tokens. The use of 'pixel' is likely a loose metaphor, not a technical error. However, without careful reading, one might mistakenly believe the model uses RGB or grayscale pixels rather than feature map responses. Given that this could mislead a reader unfamiliar with hybrid architectures, and the quotes around 'pixel' imply awareness of looseness but not correction, it remains a mild ambiguity.

comments.title: Inconsistency in model name in figure caption reference
comments.quote: Figure 2 decomposes the VTAB tasks into their respective groups, and compares to previous SOTA methods on this benchmark: BiT, VIVI – a ResNet co-trained on ImageNet and Youtube (Tschannen et al., 2020), and S4L – supervised plus semi-supervised learning on ImageNet (Zhai et al., 2019a).
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'VIVI-Ex-100% (R5 0x3)' in the figure legend, but writes 'VIVI' in the main text. While it's reasonable to treat 'VIVI' as shorthand for 'VIVI-Ex-100%', the figure uses the full name, and the model specification (R50x3) would typically denote a ResNet50 with width multiplier 3. However, 'R5 0x3' in the figure likely results from an OCR error (space inserted between '5' and '0'), correctly meaning 'R50x3'. The paper doesn't define VIVI or its architecture in the provided context, but since it is cited and described at a high level, this is not a major omission. The issue lies only in the mismatch between the figure label and its textual reference, but given that both refer to the same model family and the context clarifies it as a ResNet variant, the inconsistency is minor and resolvable from context.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in table column header: 'EffcientNet-L2'
comments.quote: |(ViT-H/14)|(ViT-L/16)|(ViT-L/16)|(ResNet152x4)|(EffcientNet-L2)|
comments.explanation: The name 'EffcientNet-L2' appears to contain a spelling error: 'Effcient' instead of 'Efficient'. Given that 'EfficientNet' is a well-known model family (Tan & Le, 2019), this is very likely an OCR artifact—especially since the rest of the paper is cleanly typeset. The intended model is almost certainly EfficientNet-L2, a known large-scale variant. No confusion arises because 'Effcient' differs from any known model naming scheme, and context (Noisy Student, which uses EfficientNets) supports this. Therefore, while technically incorrect as written, it does not impair understanding and would be instantly corrected by any domain-aware reader. Thus, the severity is minimal and clearly OCR-induced.

comments.title: Unclear justification for higher-resolution fine-tuning improving performance
comments.quote: Note that the ImageNet pre-trained models are also fine-tuned, but again on ImageNet. This is because the resolution increase during fine-tuning improves the performance.
comments.explanation: The claim that increasing resolution during fine-tuning improves performance is presented as a given, with no reference or explanation beyond prior work (Touvron et al., 2019; Kolesnikov et al., 2020) cited implicitly. In the context of ViT, this is expected due to position interpolation, which is described in Section 3.2. However, in this footnote, the authors imply a general improvement from resolution scaling even for baseline ResNets, which would not automatically benefit from 2D-aware interpolation. The paper does not clarify whether ResNets were also trained or adapted under the same resolution regime. Without such clarification, a reader might assume all models benefit equally from higher-resolution fine-tuning, which may not be true. While this doesn't invalidate results (Table 2 includes such settings consistently), the causal attribution ‘this is because’ may overstate the explanation without showing ablation or reference. Still, since the training setup is detailed elsewhere and likely consistent, this is a mild overstatement rather than an error.

comments.title: Inconsistent precision in accuracy values across table and text
comments.quote: The best model reaches the accuracy of 88 _._ 55% on ImageNet, 90 _._ 72% on ImageNet-ReaL, 94 _._ 55% on CIFAR-100, and 77 _._ 63% on the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.
comments.explanation: In Section 1, the authors state that the best model achieves 88.55% on ImageNet, 90.72% on ImageNet-ReaL, 94.55% on CIFAR-100, and 77.63% on VTAB. Table 2 shows the same values (with standard deviations) for ViT-H/14 under Ours-JFT, confirming these numbers. However, the text presents these as definitive achievements, while Table 2 shows standard deviations (e.g., ±0.04), and the footnote mentions these are averaged over three runs. The prose description omits error bars, which could mislead a reader into thinking the results are deterministic. This is not a severe issue since Table 2 corrects the impression, and such summary statements are common in abstracts and introductions. The precision matches (two decimal places), so no numerical error occurs. Still, the lack of uncertainty qualification in the initial claim is a minor overstatement.

comments.title: Inconsistent model notation: 'ViT-b/32' likely a typo for 'ViT-B/32'
comments.quote: ViT-L/32 ViT-b/32 ResNet152x2 (BiT)
comments.explanation: In Figure 4's legend, the model 'ViT-b/32' appears alongside 'ViT-L/32' and others. However, in the paper's established notation (Table 1 and Section 4.1), model sizes are capitalized: 'Base', 'Large', 'Huge', abbreviated as 'B', 'L', 'H'. Thus, 'ViT-b/32' contradicts this convention. Given that 'ViT-B/32' is mentioned just above in the same figure legend, and that no 'small' or lowercase variant is defined, this is likely an OCR error or typographical inconsistency. While the intended meaning is clear (a ViT-Base model with 32×32 patches), the lowercase 'b' breaks the paper's own notation standard. Context resolves the intent, but the inconsistency remains a minor issue.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'ViT-b' in caption without prior introduction
comments.quote: ViT-b is ViT-B with all hidden dimensions halved.
comments.explanation: The caption of Figure 4 states that 'ViT-b is ViT-B with all hidden dimensions halved.' However, this definition appears only in the caption, not in the main text or Table 1. While the label 'ViT-b/32' in the plot suggests a variation of ViT-B/32, the model 'ViT-b' is never formally defined in the method section. This risks misleading a careful reader into thinking it is a standard variant. Although the caption provides clarification, the introduction of a new model variant solely in a figure caption—especially one that modifies the architecture (halving dimensions)—is underspecified for full reproducibility. The omission of training details (e.g., whether the same hyperparameters were used) compounds this. Given that Figure 5 also includes this model, it should have been formally introduced. The issue is minor because the definition is present somewhere, but it is ambiguous upon first encounter.

comments.title: Potential mismatch between few-shot evaluation and fine-tuning claims
comments.quote: Overall, the few-shot results on ImageNet (Figure 4), as well as the low-data results on VTAB (Table 2) seem promising for very low-data transfer.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that both few-shot (Figure 4) and VTAB results support strong low-data transfer. However, VTAB results in Table 2 are from fine-tuning, not few-shot evaluation, and use 1,000 examples per task. While 'low-data' by large-scale standards, this is not the same regime as 5-shot learning. The paper elsewhere notes that few-shot provides a fast proxy, but conflating fine-tuned low-data performance with few-shot in this sentence could mislead readers into thinking ViT excels in extreme few-shot settings (e.g., 5 examples). The claim would be stronger if few-shot VTAB results were also shown. Context partially resolves this—few-shot is clearly defined as linear regression on frozen features—so the distinction is maintained technically. But the prose risks overstating alignment between the two evaluation modes. The issue is minor because both are valid transfer evaluation strategies, but the conflation is slightly misleading.

comments.title: Unjustified exclusion of Noisy Student from compute comparison
comments.quote: However, we note that pre-training efficiency may be affected not only by the architecture choice, but also other parameters, such as training schedule, optimizer, weight decay, etc. We provide a controlled study of performance vs. compute for different architectures in Section 4.4.
comments.explanation: The paper emphasizes computational efficiency as a key advantage of ViT, citing 0.68k vs 9.9k–12.3k TPUv3-core-days compared to BiT and Noisy Student. However, Noisy Student (Table 2) uses semi-supervised learning, while all ViT and BiT models use supervised learning on labeled data. This fundamental difference in training paradigm affects both compute and data efficiency. The paper acknowledges architectural and hyperparameter effects but does not address the methodological mismatch in training type. Section 4.4's 'controlled study' only includes supervised models, so Noisy Student is excluded from the direct compute-performance curve (Figure 5). This is reasonable, but the initial comparison in Table 2 includes Noisy Student in accuracy without a parallel compute-efficiency caveat. A reader might conclude ViT is more efficient than Noisy Student, but the training paradigms are not directly comparable. The authors got the reporting right (separating the models), but the juxtaposition without a stronger qualification risks implying a fairer comparison than exists. The issue is moderate because it affects the interpretation of one of the paper's central claims: compute efficiency.

comments.title: Inconsistent labeling of BiT models in Figure 2 vs. Table 2
comments.quote: BiT-L (R152x4) 90 VIVI-Ex-100% (R5 0x3)
comments.explanation: In Figure 2, the BiT model is labeled 'BiT-L (R152x4)', which aligns with Table 2 where 'BiT-L (ResNet152x4)' is compared directly with ViT variants. However, in the same figure, 'VIVI-Ex-100% (R5 0x3)' contains a likely OCR error: 'R5 0x3' with a space, clearly meant to be 'R50x3'. While 'R50x3' is not defined in the main text, the consistent pattern (e.g., R50x1, R152x2) suggests it refers to a ResNet50 with 3× width expansion. The issue is minor and likely due to OCR: the meaning is recoverable, and the model is not central to the paper's claims. But for a figure intended to summarize key comparisons, precise labeling is important. The authors correctly define BiT variants in text, so the error is localized to the figure text extraction.

comments.title: Ambiguity in model notation between ViT-b and ViT-B
comments.quote: ViT-b/32 and ViT-B/32 used in scaling comparisons with ResNets and hybrids.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned about the distinction between 'ViT-b' and 'ViT-B' because the notation appears inconsistent—both capitalization and case are used to denote model variants. In the text, 'ViT-b' is defined as a halved version of ViT-B, suggesting that the lowercase 'b' indicates architectural reduction. However, later in the paper (e.g., Figure 3), both 'ViT-B/32' and 'ViT-b/32' appear together, potentially causing confusion: is 'b' a typo for 'B', or are they deliberately distinct? Given the PAPER SUMMARY explicitly states 'ViT-b: Variant of ViT-B with all hidden dimensions halved', this clarifies the intent: 'ViT-b' is a smaller variant. The issue arises not from an actual error but from the subtle typographical distinction between 'b' and 'B', which—especially in OCR output or low-resolution displays—may be visually indistinguishable. While context resolves the meaning, this choice risks misleading readers unfamiliar with the model hierarchy or those relying on visual parsing. A more explicit notation (e.g., ViT-S for 'small') would reduce ambiguity. This is a known challenge in model naming (BERT-base vs. BERT-large), but here the use of case alone to signal a meaningful architectural difference increases cognitive load.

comments.title: Potential OCR artifact in model name 'VIVI-Ex-100%' in Figure 2 caption
comments.quote: VIVI-Ex-100% (R5 0x3)
comments.explanation: At first glance, 'VIVI-Ex-100%' appears to be a model name, but given that it does not appear in the main text or references and seems out of place among BiT, S4L, and ViT, I suspected an OCR error. Checking standard literature, I recalled that Tschannen et al. (2020) introduced 'V-IMP', not 'VIVI'. Upon closer inspection, the surrounding text cites Tschannen et al. (2020), and given the model format (R50x3), a plausible original is 'VIT' or 'ViT', but 'VIVI' is unlikely. Considering OCR tendencies, 'ViT' could be misread as 'VIVI' if characters are fused or poorly segmented—especially if rendered in a narrow font. The 'Ex' may stem from 'Rex' (as in RegNetX?) or simply noise. More plausibly, the intended text is 'ViT-R50x3' or similar, but given that R50x3 is a ResNet variant, it’s more likely that 'V' is a misread of 'B', yielding 'BIT'—but BiT is already listed. Alternatively, it could be 'RVID' or 'R50'. Given that Tschannen et al. (2020) co-train ResNet on YouTube, and given the model name 'VIVI' has no prior, I conclude this is likely an OCR misread. The most plausible reconstruction is 'BiT-R50x3' or 'R50x3', but since BiT-L already appears, it’s possible the intended name is 'VTAB' or a misread of 'ViT'. However, without the original figure, we cannot confirm. Since the error does not impact the paper’s central claims and appears only in the figure caption (possibly a rendering artifact), and since the authors’ intent is to cite Tschannen et al. (2020), I flag this only as a minor issue related to OCR degradation rather than author error.

comments.title: Misleading axis label in Figure 3 regarding pre-training dataset sizes
comments.quote: Number of JFT pre-training samples
comments.explanation: In the caption of the second plot of Figure 3, the x-axis is labeled 'Number of JFT pre-training samples' with tick marks at 10M, 30M, 100M, and 300M. However, earlier in Section 4.3, the authors state they pre-trained on ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M—suggesting three distinct datasets, not subsampled versions of JFT. This initially suggests a contradiction: are they subsampling JFT or using different datasets? Checking the main text and context, the authors clarify in Section 4.3: 'First, we pre-train ViT models on datasets of increasing size: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M.' This implies the x-axis should reflect dataset identity, not sample count. However, they then say: 'To further understand the data dependence, we also train on increasing subsets of JFT', which is shown in the second plot. Thus, context resolves the issue: the left plot shows performance across datasets, the right plot across JFT subsets. The confusion arises because the first part of the section describes increasing dataset size (cross-dataset), while the second plot is within JFT (sub-sampled). While technically correct, the ordering and lack of explicit segmentation between 'dataset comparison' and 'in-dataset scaling' could mislead a careful reader into thinking ImageNet and ImageNet-21k are subsampled from JFT. The labeling is accurate if the reader recognizes this is the JFT-only ablation, but the transition is abrupt. This could be improved by clearer segmentation or axis labeling (e.g., 'Pre-training on JFT subsets'), but given it is clarified later, the issue is minor.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of ViT model sizes in figures vs. definitions
comments.quote: ViT-L/32, ViT-B/32, ViT-H/14, ViT-B/16
comments.explanation: The model notation appears consistent overall, but a subtle issue arises in Figure 3 (right plot), where 'ViT-B/32' and 'ViT-b/32' are both plotted. The PAPER SUMMARY defines 'ViT-b' as a halved variant of ViT-B. This suggests 'ViT-b/32' is a smaller model than 'ViT-B/32', which is correct. However, Table 1 only lists ViT-Base, -Large, and -Huge—no 'ViT-b'. While this may be a minor omission, the definition is made clearly in the summary, so it's not an error. The potential issue is reproducibility: without specifying the exact parameters of ViT-b (e.g., dimensions halved—does that mean D=384?), a reader might struggle. However, this is covered in the summary as 'all hidden dimensions halved', so for a typical reader, the specification is sufficient. The notation is used consistently in figures only if one distinguishes 'B' and 'b', which—again—depends on rendering. Given that the authors provide a clear definition and usage is internal, this is not a major flaw. Still, it highlights mild underspecification in the model card (Table 1) by omitting ViT-b, but since it's derived, it's acceptable.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents a remarkably clear and compelling introduction, effectively positioning its work at the intersection of dominant trends in NLP and computer vision. The writing is of exceptional quality, from the memorable title that encapsulates the core idea to the well-structured abstract and the narrative-driven introduction. The authors do an excellent job of establishing the existing paradigm, identifying the limitations of prior hybrid approaches, and clearly stating their novel contribution: a pure Transformer applied directly to sequences of image patches. The most significant aspect of the paper's premise, and its most profound challenge to the field, is the central thesis that "large scale training trumps inductive bias." This bold assertion, which directly questions the necessity of the translation equivariance and locality inherent in CNNs, is the critical claim that the remainder of the paper must substantiate with rigorous empirical evidence. A key point of scrutiny will be the detailed validation of this claim and the precise quantification of the stated advantage in "substantially fewer computational resources."

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in output class token indexing
comments.quote: ... prepend a learnable embedding to the sequence of embedded patches ($z^0_0 = x_{class}$), whose state at the output of the Transformer encoder ($z^0_L$) serves as the image representation $y$ (Eq. 4).
comments.explanation: The text defines the input class token embedding as $z^0_0$. Assuming standard notation where the superscript denotes the layer index (0 for input) and the subscript denotes the token index (0 for the class token), the variable representing the output of the Transformer encoder should logically be $z^L_0$ (layer $L$, token 0). However, the text writes $z^0_L$, which suggests layer 0 and token index $L$. This contradicts the textual description of it being the 'output of the Transformer encoder'. While the surrounding text clarifies the intent, the mathematical notation is inconsistent with the definition of $z^0_0$ and standard conventions.

comments.title: Inconsistency between text and table regarding ViT-L/16 performance on VTAB
comments.quote: The smaller ViT-L/16 model pre-trained on JFT-300M outperforms BiT-L (which is pre-trained on the same dataset) on all tasks, while requiring substantially less computational resources to train.
comments.explanation: The text explicitly claims that the ViT-L/16 model outperforms BiT-L on 'all tasks'. However, Table 2 presents the VTAB (19 tasks) scores where BiT-L achieves 76.29% and ViT-L/16 achieves 76.28%. According to the mean values reported in the table, BiT-L slightly outperforms ViT-L/16 on this specific benchmark, contradicting the 'all tasks' claim. While the difference (0.01%) is negligible and likely within statistical variance (std. dev. for ViT-L/16 is ±0.46), the textual statement is factually inaccurate based on the provided data.

comments.title: Minor editorial errors in scaling study section
comments.quote: total dowsampling ratio... R50x2 R101x1... pre-trained... pretrained
comments.explanation: The scaling study section contains several minor text errors. First, there is a typo where 'dowsampling' is used instead of 'downsampling'. Second, there is a missing comma in the ResNet model listing between 'R50x2' and 'R101x1'. Third, the hyphenation of 'pre-trained' is inconsistent, appearing both with and without the hyphen throughout the section.

progressive_original__glm-4.6.label: Progressive Original (glm-4.6)
progressive_original__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents a remarkably clear and compelling introduction, effectively positioning its work at the intersection of dominant trends in NLP and computer vision. The writing is of exceptional quality, from the memorable title that encapsulates the core idea to the well-structured abstract and the narrative-driven introduction. The authors do an excellent job of establishing the existing paradigm, identifying the limitations of prior hybrid approaches, and clearly stating their novel contribution: a pure Transformer applied directly to sequences of image patches. The most significant aspect of the paper's premise, and its most profound challenge to the field, is the central thesis that "large scale training trumps inductive bias." This bold assertion, which directly questions the necessity of the translation equivariance and locality inherent in CNNs, is the critical claim that the remainder of the paper must substantiate with rigorous empirical evidence. A key point of scrutiny will be the detailed validation of this claim and the precise quantification of the stated advantage in "substantially fewer computational resources."

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in output class token indexing
comments.quote: ... prepend a learnable embedding to the sequence of embedded patches ($z^0_0 = x_{class}$), whose state at the output of the Transformer encoder ($z^0_L$) serves as the image representation $y$ (Eq. 4).
comments.explanation: The text defines the input class token embedding as $z^0_0$. Assuming standard notation where the superscript denotes the layer index (0 for input) and the subscript denotes the token index (0 for the class token), the variable representing the output of the Transformer encoder should logically be $z^L_0$ (layer $L$, token 0). However, the text writes $z^0_L$, which suggests layer 0 and token index $L$. This contradicts the textual description of it being the 'output of the Transformer encoder'. While the surrounding text clarifies the intent, the mathematical notation is inconsistent with the definition of $z^0_0$ and standard conventions.

comments.title: Inconsistency between text and table regarding ViT-L/16 performance on VTAB
comments.quote: The smaller ViT-L/16 model pre-trained on JFT-300M outperforms BiT-L (which is pre-trained on the same dataset) on all tasks, while requiring substantially less computational resources to train.
comments.explanation: The text explicitly claims that the ViT-L/16 model outperforms BiT-L on 'all tasks'. However, Table 2 presents the VTAB (19 tasks) scores where BiT-L achieves 76.29% and ViT-L/16 achieves 76.28%. According to the mean values reported in the table, BiT-L slightly outperforms ViT-L/16 on this specific benchmark, contradicting the 'all tasks' claim. While the difference (0.01%) is negligible and likely within statistical variance (std. dev. for ViT-L/16 is ±0.46), the textual statement is factually inaccurate based on the provided data.

comments.title: Minor notation inconsistency in model variant listing
comments.quote: 6 Vision Transformers, ViT-B/32, B/16, L/32, L/16, pre-trained for 7 epochs
comments.explanation: In the scaling study section, the authors list '6 Vision Transformers, ViT-B/32, B/16, L/32, L/16, pre-trained for 7 epochs'. The notation is inconsistent - some variants include the 'ViT-' prefix (ViT-B/32) while others don't (B/16, L/32, L/16). While it's clear from context that these are all ViT variants, and this is likely just an abbreviation for brevity, it creates a minor inconsistency in notation. The same issue appears in the hybrids listing: 'R50+ViT-B/32, B/16, L/32, L/16 pretrained for 7 epochs'. This is a minor issue that doesn't affect understanding but could be confusing for readers trying to reproduce the exact model configurations.

comments.title: Typo in scaling study section

The meaning is still clear from context, but this is a spelling error that should be corrected.

comments.title: Inconsistent hyphenation in 'pre-trained'
comments.quote: R50+ViT-L/16 pre-trained for 14 epochs (for hybrids, the number at the end of the model name stands not for the patch size, but for the total dowsampling ratio in the ResNet backbone).
comments.explanation: In the scaling study section, there's inconsistent hyphenation of 'pre-trained'. This is a minor inconsistency that doesn't affect understanding but should be standardized for consistency throughout the paper.

comments.explanation: In the scaling study section, there's a missing comma between 'R50x2' and 'R101x1' in the list of ResNet models. This is a minor formatting error that could momentarily confuse readers, but the meaning is still clear from context.
